report_name: TeamPCP Weaponizes npm with Malicious Bitwarden CLI in Sophisticated Supply Chain Attack

threat_actors.name: TeamPCP
threat_actors.description: A sophisticated threat actor group that conducted a supply chain attack on the npm ecosystem by publishing a malicious package impersonating the Bitwarden CLI. The group's objective was widespread credential harvesting from developer environments.
threat_actors.goals: Widespread credential harvesting from developer environments, including local files, CI/CD systems, and major cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

attack_patterns.name: Supply Chain Compromise
attack_patterns.description: The attack was initiated by publishing a malicious package, `@bitwarden/cli`, to the public npm registry, compromising the software supply chain for developers who installed it.

attack_patterns.name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_patterns.description: The malware propagated by using stolen npm and GitHub tokens to find, backdoor, and republish other npm packages the compromised user had access to, turning victims into distributors.

attack_patterns.name: Trusted Relationship
attack_patterns.description: Attackers exploited the trust developers place in the npm ecosystem and in legitimate tool names like Bitwarden CLI to trick them into installing the malicious package.

attack_patterns.name: User Execution
attack_patterns.description: The attack relies on a developer executing `npm install` on the malicious package, which triggers the payload.

attack_patterns.name: Malicious File
attack_patterns.description: The malicious npm package `@bitwarden/cli` acts as a malicious file that, when installed, executes a credential harvesting payload.

attack_patterns.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: JavaScript
attack_patterns.description: The malware's payload was a heavily obfuscated JavaScript file (`bw_setup.js`) executed by Node.js via an npm `preinstall` hook.

attack_patterns.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
attack_patterns.description: The attackers used multiple obfuscation layers, including bundling legitimate SDKs, a custom character substitution cipher for strings like the C2 domain, and string manipulation to hinder analysis.

attack_patterns.name: Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
attack_patterns.description: The malware payload scoured the local filesystem for credentials in files such as `.npmrc`, `.git-credentials`, `.bash_history`, and SSH keys.

attack_patterns.name: Credentials from Password Stores: Cloud Secrets Management Stores
attack_patterns.description: The malware used bundled SDKs to programmatically enumerate and retrieve secrets from AWS Systems Manager, Azure Key Vault, and Google Cloud Secret Manager.

attack_patterns.name: Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: Harvested credentials and data were exfiltrated to the command-and-control server `audit.checkmarx.cx` over web protocols.

malware.name: @bitwarden/cli
malware.description: A malicious npm package (version 2026.4.0) that impersonates the legitimate Bitwarden CLI tool. It contains a multi-stage, obfuscated payload for credential harvesting and has worm-like capabilities to backdoor and republish other npm packages.

malware.name: Shai-Hulud
malware.description: A worm from 2025 whose capabilities inspired the propagation mechanism of the malicious @bitwarden/cli package used by TeamPCP.

tools.name: npm
tools.description: The npm package manager was the primary vector for the supply chain attack. The attackers published a malicious package to the public npm registry and used `preinstall` hooks for execution.

tools.name: GitHub CLI
tools.description: The malware executes the `gh auth token` command to steal the active GitHub CLI token from the compromised developer's environment.

campaigns.name: TeamPCP npm Malware Campaign
campaigns.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack campaign by TeamPCP targeting the npm ecosystem. The campaign used a malicious package impersonating the Bitwarden CLI to harvest developer credentials and propagate through the software supply chain.

identities.name: Bitwarden
identities.description: A legitimate password manager company whose command-line tool was impersonated by the malicious npm package `@bitwarden/cli`.

identities.name: Checkmarx
identities.description: A security tooling vendor targeted by TeamPCP. The C2 domain `audit.checkmarx.cx` also impersonated this company.

identities.name: Trivy
identities.description: A security tooling vendor targeted by TeamPCP as part of a broader campaign.

identities.name: LiteLLM
identities.description: A security tooling vendor targeted by TeamPCP as part of a broader campaign.

identities.name: npm
identities.description: The public software registry targeted by the supply chain attack.

identities.name: GitHub
identities.description: The platform from which the malware stole authentication tokens to propagate to other software packages.

identities.name: Unit 42
identities.description: The security research group from Palo Alto Networks that uncovered and reported on the TeamPCP campaign.

infrastructure.name: TeamPCP C2 Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Centralized command-and-control infrastructure at audit.checkmarx.cx used to receive credentials harvested by the malware.

indicators.name: TeamPCP Malicious npm Package C2
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: TeamPCP Malicious npm Package C2 DNS Query
id: 2b9c1d8e-4f5a-4b6c-8d9e-1f2a3b4c5d6e
status: experimental
description: Detects DNS queries to the C2 domain audit.checkmarx.cx used in the TeamPCP npm supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-25
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.command_and_control
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate traffic to Checkmarx services, though this specific subdomain is suspicious.
level: high
logsource:
  category: dns_query
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    QueryName: 'audit.checkmarx.cx'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects network traffic to the command-and-control domain used by the malicious @bitwarden/cli npm package.

indicators.name: TeamPCP Malicious Script Detection
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: TeamPCP Malicious Script File Creation
id: 8e1f2a3b-4c5d-4e6f-8a9b-0c1d2e3f4a5b
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of malicious JavaScript files associated with the TeamPCP npm supply chain attack.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-25
tags:
  - attack.t1204.002
  - attack.execution
falsepositives:
  - Unlikely, but legitimate files could share these names.
level: high
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '\bw_setup.js'
      - '\mcpAddon.js'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects the presence of malicious script files used in the TeamPCP npm supply chain attack.

indicators.name: Suspicious GitHub Token Theft by Node.js
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious GitHub Token Theft by Node.js Process
id: 5d6e7f8a-9b0c-4d2e-8f4a-5b6c7d8e9f0a
status: experimental
description: Detects a Node.js process spawning the GitHub CLI to steal an authentication token, a TTP used by the TeamPCP npm malware.
references:
  - https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/monitoring-npm-supply-chain-attacks/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-04-25
tags:
  - attack.t1552.001
  - attack.credential_access
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate build scripts or developer tools that programmatically interact with the GitHub CLI.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\node.exe'
    Image|endswith: '\gh.exe'
    CommandLine|contains: 'auth token'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects a Node.js process executing the GitHub CLI (`gh auth token`) to steal credentials, a behavior observed in the TeamPCP campaign.

relationships.target_name: Credentials from Password Stores: Cloud Secrets Management Stores

relationships.source_name: Suspicious GitHub Token Theft by Node.js
